title: Number of Islands
code: int rows = grid.Length, cols = grid[0].Length, count = 0;
if (r < 0 || r >= rows || c < 0 || c >= cols || grid[r][c] != '1') return;
grid[r][c] = '0';                 // mark visited by sinking it

frames.caption: Scan the grid cell by cell. Each '1' (land) we have not yet visited starts a new island; flood-fill sinks the rest of it so we count each island only once.
frames.note: Number of Islands treats the grid as an implicit graph: every land cell is a vertex, and orthogonally adjacent land cells share an edge. The outer double loop is a linear scan; the only real work happens when it discovers fresh land, where a flood fill (DFS here) claims the whole connected component before the scan moves on.

frames.caption: (0,0) is unvisited land → new island #1. count++ , then flood-fill from here.
frames.note: This cell is land and has not been visited, so it is the first cell we reach of a brand-new island. We bump the count by one, then flood-fill from here to claim every cell connected to it. Each island is counted exactly once — at the single moment its scan first lands on it — because the flood that follows sinks the rest so the outer scan never re-counts them.

frames.caption: Sink (0,0) — overwrite this land with water. It is now permanently visited.
frames.note: Sink this land cell by overwriting it with water ('0'). Mutating the grid IS our visited-marker — no separate seen[] array. A cell that is already '0' (water, a wall, or land we just sank) fails the grid[r][c] != '1' guard and returns immediately, so each cell is entered at most once. That cap is exactly why the whole algorithm is O(rows*cols).

frames.caption: Sink (1,0) — overwrite this land with water. It is now permanently visited.
frames.note: Sink this land cell by overwriting it with water ('0'). Mutating the grid IS our visited-marker — no separate seen[] array. A cell that is already '0' (water, a wall, or land we just sank) fails the grid[r][c] != '1' guard and returns immediately, so each cell is entered at most once. That cap is exactly why the whole algorithm is O(rows*cols).

frames.caption: Sink (0,1) — overwrite this land with water. It is now permanently visited.
frames.note: Sink this land cell by overwriting it with water ('0'). Mutating the grid IS our visited-marker — no separate seen[] array. A cell that is already '0' (water, a wall, or land we just sank) fails the grid[r][c] != '1' guard and returns immediately, so each cell is entered at most once. That cap is exactly why the whole algorithm is O(rows*cols).

frames.caption: Island #1 is fully sunk (3 cells). The four-direction recursion has dead-ended in every direction.
frames.note: From the current cell we recurse to its four neighbors — down, up, right, left — each guarded by the same bounds + land check. The recursion follows the connected component like DFS on an implicit graph: the grid never stores edges, the four-direction step IS the edge. When all four directions dead-end, this island is fully sunk.

frames.caption: (0,1)..(0,3) are water — nothing to start here, keep scanning.
frames.note: The outer scan steps over a water ('0') cell. It is either original ocean or part of an island we already sank, so there is nothing to start here — keep scanning. Every cell is visited once by this scan, and land cells are additionally entered once by a flood fill, keeping the total work linear in the grid size.

When all four directions dead-end, this island is fully sunk.

frames.caption: Scan complete. We found 3 islands.
frames.note: The scan has swept every cell. The count went up once per island and never double-counted, because each flood fill sank its island to water before the scan could reach the rest of it. Total cost: O(rows*cols) time (each cell touched a constant number of times) and O(rows*cols) worst-case recursion depth for one giant snaking island.
